title: Day 10: AI Business Strategy — SWOT, Roadmaps & GTM Plans

steps.title: AI as Your Strategy Consultant
steps.body:
Strategy documents are expensive. A good SWOT analysis from a consultant runs $2,000-5,000. A go-to-market plan? $5,000-15,000.

Today you'll learn to produce strategy-grade documents with AI. Not by replacing the strategic thinking — but by accelerating every step of the process.

The operator's approach: AI generates the full draft. You shape, challenge, and refine. The result is better than either could produce alone.

Strategy with AI follows the broad → narrow → refined pattern:

1. Broad exploration: 'What are all the possible strategic approaches for [situation]?'
2. Narrow to best options: 'Of those 10, which 3 have the highest probability of success?'
3. Refine with specifics: 'For option 2, build a detailed 90-day execution plan with milestones, resources, and risks.'

Start wide. Then focus. Then execute.

steps.title: Building a GTM Plan with AI
steps.prompt:
Prompt sequence for a complete go-to-market plan:

Stage 1 — Market Analysis:
'Analyze the [industry] market. Size it, identify key segments, list major competitors and their market share. Output as a structured market overview.'

Stage 2 — Positioning:
'Based on our product [description], what positioning would differentiate us from competitors? Propose 3 positioning angles with pros and cons for each.'

Stage 3 — Tactical Plan:
'For positioning option 2, build a 90-day GTM plan covering: target accounts, channel strategy, pricing approach, launch timeline, success metrics.'

Stage 4 — Risk Assessment:
'Review the GTM plan. What are the top 5 risks? For each, provide a mitigation strategy.'

Stage 5 — Executive Summary:
'Synthesize everything into a one-page executive summary suitable for presenting to stakeholders.'

Output: A complete GTM document that would cost $10K from a consultant. Produced in 90 minutes of guided prompting.

steps.question: The best strategy documents come from asking AI a single comprehensive question.
feedback: Correct. Great strategy emerges from iteration — broad exploration first, then narrowing, then deep refinement. One-shot prompts produce shallow analysis.
Not quite. Strategy requires multiple rounds. Start broad to explore possibilities, then narrow and refine. Each stage builds on the previous one.

steps.title: Design a Strategic Analysis
steps.instruction:
Pick a real business challenge you're facing (or one you're interested in). Design a 3-stage AI strategy session:

Challenge: [One sentence — what strategic problem are you solving?]

Stage 1 — Explore (Broad): [Ask AI to survey all possible approaches]
Stage 2 — Narrow: [Ask AI to evaluate and select the best options]
Stage 3 — Execute: [Ask AI to build a detailed plan for the chosen approach]
steps.placeholder:
Challenge: Launching a paid AI course for real estate agents

Stage 1 — 'Survey the market for AI education in real estate. Who offers courses? What's the pricing range? What topics get the most engagement? Identify gaps in current offerings.'

Stage 2 — 'Based on the market analysis, evaluate 3 positioning strategies: (a) beginner crash course, (b) advanced agent automation, (c) AI tools bundle membership. Which has the highest revenue potential vs. easiest to build?'

Stage 3 — 'For the best option from stage 2, build a complete launch plan: target audience, pricing tiers, content outline, marketing channels, partnership opportunities, 60-day launch timeline.'

steps.title: Day 10 Complete!
steps.body:
You can now produce strategy-grade documents with AI: market analysis, competitive positioning, GTM plans, and risk assessments.

Tomorrow: Building a complete AI-powered marketing machine — audience research, email sequences, and campaign design.
